input.query_title: Turing patterns in network-organized activator-inhibitor systems
input.query_abstract: Turing instability in activator-inhibitor systems provides a paradigm of nonequilibrium pattern formation; it has been extensively investigated for biological and chemical processes. Turing pattern formation should furthermore be possible in network-organized systems, such as cellular networks in morphogenesis and ecological metapopulations with dispersal connections between habitats, but investigations have so far been restricted to regular lattices and small networks. Here we report the first systematic investigation of Turing patterns in large random networks, which reveals their striking difference from the known classical behavior. In such networks, Turing instability leads to spontaneous differentiation of the network nodes into activator-rich and activator-low groups, but ordered periodic structures never develop. Only a subset of nodes having close degrees (numbers of links) undergoes differentiation, with its characteristic degree obeying a simple general law. Strong nonlinear restructuring process leads to multiple coexisting states and hysteresis effects. The final stationary patterns can be well understood in the framework of the mean-field approximation for network dynamics.
input.hyp_generic_object: coupled agglomeration and congestion indices across a network of trading regions
input.hyp_abstract: Why do symmetric spatial economies spontaneously polarize into prosperous cores and lagging peripheries even without underlying locational advantages? We model each region with two interacting state variables: a locally self-reinforcing measure of firm and skilled-labor agglomeration, and a countervailing congestion-and-cost index, coupled across an inter-regional network whose links encode trade and commuting flows. The network's weighted Laplacian governs spatial redistribution, replacing the continuous-space transport operators of standard core-periphery theory. Linearizing about the symmetric equilibrium in which all regions are identical and expanding disturbances in the Laplacian eigenbasis gives a discrete growth-rate spectrum indexed by network modes. When cost pressures propagate across the trade network markedly faster than agglomeration benefits, the homogeneous configuration, robust to uniform shocks, loses stability along particular modes, and the system self-organizes into distinct high-density and low-density regional classes rather than a smoothly graded gradient. Strikingly, only regions of intermediate connectivity tip, and we obtain an analytic threshold for this participating connectivity. Nonlinearity produces multiple coexisting spatial equilibria and path dependence, so identical fundamentals can support divergent regional hierarchies depending on history. A mean-field approximation over connectivity strata predicts the emergent core share, offering a tractable account of endogenous polarization on realistic mobility networks and clarifying when integration policy amplifies rather than reduces disparities.
input.candidate_title: Correlation, Network and Multifractal Analysis of Global Financial Indices
input.candidate_abstract: We apply RMT, Network and MF-DFA methods to investigate correlation, network and multifractal properties of 20 global financial indices. We compare results before and during the financial crisis of 2008 respectively. We find that the network method gives more useful information about the formation of clusters as compared to results obtained from eigenvectors corresponding to second largest eigenvalue and these sectors are formed on the basis of geographical location of indices. At threshold 0.6, indices corresponding to Americas, Europe and Asia/Pacific disconnect and form different clusters before the crisis but during the crisis, indices corresponding to Americas and Europe are combined together to form a cluster while the Asia/Pacific indices forms another cluster. By further increasing the value of threshold to 0.9, European countries France, Germany and UK constitute the most tightly linked markets. We study multifractal properties of global financial indices and find that financial indices corresponding to Americas and Europe almost lie in the same range of degree of multifractality as compared to other indices. India, South Korea, Hong Kong are found to be near the degree of multifractality of indices corresponding to Americas and Europe. A large variation in the degree of multifractality in Egypt, Indonesia, Malaysia, Taiwan and Singapore may be a reason that when we increase the threshold in financial network these countries first start getting disconnected at low threshold from the correlation network of financial indices. We fit Binomial Multifractal Model (BMFM) to these financial markets.
